output.answer:
## The Local Economy: Mixed Signals, With Tension Between Surface Strength and Underlying Fragility

The memos do not tell a single story. They point in genuinely different directions depending on which indicator you look at — and the picture shifts depending on the time period covered.

---

### Where the Memos Suggest Strength

**Wages and consumer spending** are the clearest bright spots. MAR-023 reports average weekly wages of $1,415, up 6.4% year-over-year — explicitly noted as outpacing inflation. FEB-016 shows city revenues trending 7.81% above the prior year, with the Consolidated Tax (a proxy for retail and economic activity) up 7.5%. MAR-001 adds that Washoe County taxable sales hit $1.1 billion in November, up 10.4% year-over-year. These figures, taken together, suggest real spending power and economic activity are holding up.

**Tourism** also looks strong in the most recent data. MAR-023 reports 307,686 visitors in January 2026, up 12.3% year-over-year, with hotel occupancy at 58.9% (+5.3 points). JAN-014 showed a more modest +5% visitor gain in November 2025, so the trend appears to be accelerating, not fading.

---

### Where the Memos Suggest Weakness or Risk

**Consumer confidence is the most alarming counter-signal.** JAN-014 reported a Consumer Confidence Index of 89.1 — already down 20.4 points year-over-year. MAR-001 then recorded a further drop to 84.5, described as the lowest since May 2014 — a 14.7-point year-over-year decline. By MAR-023, the index had partially recovered to 91.2, but the trajectory over the three-month window is volatile and the level remains historically depressed. Consumers are spending, but they are not confident — a combination that historically precedes pullback.

**Job market signals are deteriorating beneath the surface.** MAR-001 flags that job openings fell 25.6% year-over-year — a leading indicator of future hiring weakness, even if current unemployment (4.0% in MAR-001, 4.2% in JAN-014) looks stable. MAR-023 adds two WARN Act notices: Water Safety Corp (35 jobs, April 1) and SK Food Group (209 jobs, end of April) — meaning roughly 244 layoffs are already scheduled and confirmed, not speculative.

**External risks are explicitly flagged.** MAR-023 names trade policy uncertainty and geopolitical tensions (specifically the Iran conflict) as sources of growing uncertainty. These are not local factors the city can control.

---

### The Housing Picture: A Specific Tension

Home values are slightly negative year-over-year across all three monthly updates (JAN-014: -0.2%; MAR-001: -0.4%; MAR-023: -0.4%), suggesting the ownership market is soft. But rents have risen for 12 consecutive months (MAR-023), reaching $1,769/month (+8.4% YoY). This divergence — falling purchase prices, rising rents — suggests affordability pressure on renters is intensifying even as the for-sale market cools.

---

### What the Memos Do NOT Resolve

The homelessness and service-request memos (FEB-008, FEB-009, MAR-016) don't directly address economic conditions, but the volume of outreach activity and enforcement actions in December 2025 is consistent with ongoing economic stress at the margins of the population — though the memos do not draw that connection explicitly.

---

### Bottom Line

The memos suggest an economy that is **expanding on the surface but softening underneath**. Spending, wages, tourism, and city revenues are all up. But consumer confidence is near a 12-year low (MAR-001), job openings are falling sharply (MAR-001), confirmed layoffs are coming (MAR-023), and external risks are rising (MAR-023). The most honest read across these documents is: **the current numbers look decent, but the forward-looking indicators are flashing caution.**

cited_sources.title: December 2025 Clean & Safe Team Homeless Actions & Outcomes
Reno Direct & Service Requests Report — January 2026
